coredata.dc:title: Nickel-doped carbon dots with enhanced and tunable multicolor fluorescence emission for multicolor light-emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Yunhui
Li, Jiurong
Zhao, Xiujian
Gong, Xiao
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, which have received a lot of interest because of their excellent properties, simple synthesis and wide applications. However, facial and fast synthesis of CDs with tunable multicolor emission that can be used for lighting still remains a challenge. Here, through a simple one-step solvothermal method, nickel-doped CDs with tunable fluorescence emission are successfully prepared. By optimizing the experimental conditions, fluorescence emission of the as-prepared CDs can be adjusted within a certain wavelength range (513 nm–612 nm), that is, from green to red. The particle size of the CDs also increases from 2 nm to 8 nm. The introduction of Ni can successfully enhance the fluorescence intensity and cause an obvious redshift. In addition, by mixing polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) with the multicolor nickel-doped CDs (Ni-MCDs), the self-quenching effect of solid-state fluorescence can be effectively avoided. Based on this, green, yellow and red light-emitting diodes (LEDs) with high performance can be constructed by integrating CDs/PVP composites with different colors on top of the UV chip, demonstrating their superior potential for solid-state lighting applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Tunable multicolor emission
Ni doping
Light-emitting diodes

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of Ni-MCDs.

TEM and HRTEM images of (a) CDs1-DMF-1 h, (b) CDs2-MeOH-3 h, (c) CDs3-MeOH-13 h. Particle size distribution of (d) CDs1-DMF-1 h, (e) CDs2-MeOH-3 h, (f) CDs3-MeOH-13 h. TEM and HRTEM images of (g) CDs4-DMF-5 h, (h) CDs5-DMF-20 h, (i) CDs6-DMF-20 h 8. Particle size distribution of (j) CDs4-DMF-5 h, (k) CDs5-DMF-20 h, (l) CDs6-DMF-20 h 8.

(a) Full XPS spectrum of CDs1-DMF-1 h. (b) High-resolution C1s spectrum of CDs1-DMF-1 h. (c) High-resolution N1s spectrum of CDs1-DMF-1 h. (d) Full XPS spectrum of CDs4-DMF-5 h. (e) High-resolution C1s spectrum of CDs4-DMF-5 h. (f) High-resolution N1s spectrum of CDs4-DMF-5 h. (g) Full XPS spectrum of CDs6-DMF-20 h 8. (h) High-resolution C1s spectrum of CDs6-DMF-20 h 8. (i) High-resolution N1s spectrum of CDs6-DMF-20 h 8.

(a) Absorption spectra of CDs1-DMF-1 h to CDs6-DMF-20 h 8. (b) Emission spectra of CDs1-DMF-1 h to CDs6-DMF-20 h 8. (c) Excitation spectra of CDs1-DMF-1 h to CDs6-DMF-20 h 8. (d) Emission spectra of CDs1-DMF-1 h and CDs1 under optimal excitation. (e) Emission spectra of CDs2- MeOH -3 h and CDs2 under optimal excitation. (f) Emission spectra of CDs3-MeOH-13 h and CDs3 under optimal excitation. (g) Emission spectra of CDs4-DMF-5 h and CDs4 under optimal excitation. (h) Emission spectra of CDs5-DMF-20 h and CDs5 under optimal excitation. (g) Emission spectra of CDs6-DMF-20 h 8 and CDs6 under optimal excitation.

(a) CIE coordinates and optical spectrum of green LED. (b) CIE coordinates and optical spectrum of yellow LED. (c) CIE coordinates and optical spectrum of red LED. (d) Emission spectrum of green LED. (e) Emission spectrum of yellow LED. (f) Emission spectrum of red LED.

head.ce:title: Nickel-doped carbon dots with enhanced and tunable multicolor fluorescence emission for multicolor light-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan 430070, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, which have received a lot of interest because of their excellent properties, simple synthesis and wide applications. However, facial and fast synthesis of CDs with tunable multicolor emission that can be used for lighting still remains a challenge. Here, through a simple one-step solvothermal method, nickel-doped CDs with tunable fluorescence emission are successfully prepared. By optimizing the experimental conditions, fluorescence emission of the as-prepared CDs can be adjusted within a certain wavelength range (513 nm–612 nm), that is, from green to red. The particle size of the CDs also increases from 2 nm to 8 nm. The introduction of Ni can successfully enhance the fluorescence intensity and cause an obvious redshift. In addition, by mixing polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) with the multicolor nickel-doped CDs (Ni-MCDs), the self-quenching effect of solid-state fluorescence can be effectively avoided. Based on this, green, yellow and red light-emitting diodes (LEDs) with high performance can be constructed by integrating CDs/PVP composites with different colors on top of the UV chip, demonstrating their superior potential for solid-state lighting applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of carbon-based luminescent nanomaterials with a typical size less than 10 nm, which possess many special properties such as excellent photoluminescence (PL), low cost, good biocompatibility, and multiple synthesis methods [1–4]. Since their discovery in 2004 [5], CDs have received a lot of attention. At present, two methods of synthesizing CDs are mainly adopted: top-down and bottom-up. The former method mainly refers to the breaking of macromolecular carbon materials into small molecular carbon nanoparticles through certain physical and chemical approaches, including laser ablation [3,6,7], electrochemical synthesis [8], arc discharge [3,5,7], etc., while the latter method mainly refers to the synthesis and agglomeration of small molecular carbon materials into larger molecular weight carbon nanoparticles by means of certain chemical methods, such as microwave-assistant method [3,7,9,10], hydrothermal/solvothermal method [11–13], template method [3,14], chemical oxidation method [3,7,15], combustion method [16], etc.

Although the hydrothermal/solvothermal approach is quite simple and efficient, the emission of most CDs synthetized by this method is mainly blue or green fluorescence located in the short-wavelength region [17–19]. To achieve the long-wavelength fluorescent emissive CDs, numerous improvements have been developed, such as heteroatom doping [10,20–22], surface modification [23–25], polymer crosslinking [4,26], etc. Heteroatom doping is considered as the most intuitive and effective way to achieve long-wavelength fluorescence since the introduction of heteroatoms into carbon nanostructures may cause significant changes in their electronic structure, nanostructure and chemical composition due to the overlap of atomic orbitals between heteroatoms and carbon atoms, and the electron push/pull effect of heteroatoms [27]. Up to now, there are two types of heteroatom doping in the field of CDs preparation: nonmetallic element doping and metal element doping. Theoretically, the fluorescence of CDs is essentially due to the quantum confinement of the carbon structure core of sp2. The introduction of non-metallic atoms will affect the overall electrical distribution and related electronic energy levels of CDs [28]. And nonmetallic doping has been widely studied and proved to be an effective method to adjust the fluorescence and other characteristics of CDs, such as single-atom doping with N [29–31], S [32–34], P [20,35], B [36,37], F, [38,39], two elements co-doping with N/S [33,34,40–42], B/N [37,43], N/P [20], F/N [39], and three elements co-doping with N/S/B [43]. Thus, tunable multicolor CDs by nonmetallic doping have been achieved [24].

The researches on the preparations of CDs by metal element doping (Mn, Fe, Cu, Zn, Co, Mg, Ni, etc.) have also made rapid progress. Kumari et al. [22] reported the preparation of manganese-doped CDs (Mn-CDs) with dual emission based on pyromellitic acid. Pakkath et al. [10] reported a rapid, facile synthesis of ethylenediamine-functionalized transition metal ions (Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+ and Ni2+) doped CDs by one pot microwave-assisted pyrolysis to improve the fluorescence and photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY). Xue et al. [44] reported that CDs with copper and zinc bimetallic covalent doped (CuZn-CDs) showed good biocompatibility, processing catalase rather than peroxidase. Wu et al. [45] also found the synergistic effect of Zn and Cu co-doping in CDs, which could produce enhanced electron transfer and photooxidation processes in doped CDs, resulting in the improvement of catalytic performance. However, most of these reported results are only for the synthesis of monochromatic fluorescence, and there are few reports on the direct synthesis of tunable multicolor fluorescent CDs with single-metal element doping. What's more, it also reflects that the development of multicolor CDs is still in its early stage, the synthesis methods are complex (different types of carbon sources, time-consuming and laborious post-processing and complex synthesis conditions, etc.), and the luminescence mechanism is still controversial. Therefore, it remains a great challenge to design a simple and controllable CDs synthesis strategy with multicolor fluorescence (especially long-wavelength fluorescence) and to elucidate the relationship between CDs structure and tunable fluorescence. It has been reported that metal atoms are good electron donors and have more unoccupied orbitals and larger atomic radii than common nonmetallic atoms (B, N, S, P, etc.) [21]. Therefore, doping of metal atoms in CDs provides more opportunities to change the HOMO-LUMO gap of the electronic structure of CDs, so as to achieve tunable photoluminescence and high PLQY of CDs [46]. In addition, CDs with metal elements as dopants show higher absorbance in the visible region compared to undoped CDs, due to the charge transfer absorption from metal ions to graphite [30]. Thus, metal doping is considered to be a simple method to adjust and optimize the optical properties of CDs.

Here, we demonstrated a new strategy of single-metal doping for synthesizing tunable multicolor CDs. By optimizing the reaction conditions, nickel doped CDs (Ni-CDs) with multi-color luminescence from green to red can be prepared without any complex post-treatment process, using o-phenylenediamine as carbon and nitrogen source and nickel chloride hexahydrate as metal dopant. It is found that with the help of Ni2+, the dehydration and carbonization process could be controlled by reaction time so as to adjust the particle size and graphite nitrogen content of the CDs, eventually obtaining multicolor CDs. The fluorescence was greatly enhanced by the introduction of Ni, and the prepared Ni-CDs had good dispersion in solvents and uniform particle size distribution. In addition, solid-state fluorescence could be achieved by mixing the CDs with polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP). Then, Ni-CDs/PVP composite film could be covered on light-emitting diodes (LEDs) chip to construct monochrome LED. Our simple yet efficient method of preparing CDs with enhanced and tunable multicolor fluorescence emission shows huge promising for their applications in LED in the future.

o-phenylenediamine (oPD), nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2 6H2O), and polyvinylpyrrolidone (K30 PVP), were supplied by Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd., China. N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol (EtOH), methanol (MeOH), toluene (TOL), acetone (ACE), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), cyclohexanol (CYC), formamid (FOR), IPA, and 1-butanol (1-BUT), were supplied by Sinopharm Chemistry Reagent Co., Ltd., China.

Preparation of undoped CDs1: 1 mmol oPD was dispersed in 10 mL DMF. The solution was sonicated for 25 min until the mixture was completely dissolved. The mixed solution was then transferred to the reaction kettle in a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (25 mL). The autoclave was then stored in an oven at 180 °C for 1 h and cooled naturally to room temperature. The original solution was then filtered twice through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Subsequently, the solution was dialyzed for 24 h by a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 1000 Da in order to remove some unreacted reactants and other impurities. Finally, the dialyzed solution was treated by rotary evaporation, and the obtained sample was dissolved in a suitable solvent for subsequent characterization.

Preparation of undoped CDs2: The whole synthesis method was the same as that of CDs1, except that the reaction solvent and reaction time were different. And the reaction solvent and reaction time were methanol and 3 h, respectively.
Preparation of undoped CDs3：The whole synthesis method was the same as that of CDs2, except that the reaction time was 13 h.
Preparation of undoped CDs4: The whole synthesis method was the same as that of CDs1, except that the reaction time was 5 h.
Preparation of undoped CDs5: The whole synthesis method was the same as that of CDs1, except that the reaction time was 20 h.
Preparation of undoped CDs6: 8 mmol oPD were dispersed in 10 mL DMF. The other conditions were prepared in the same way as the CDs5.

The K30 PVP was chosen to construct the LED luminous film. PVP mixed with the CDs can effectively disperse the CDs to inhibit the non-radiative decay process and PL quenching caused by the aggregation of CDs. Therefore, CDs/PVP composites can be prepared to achieve solid emission of CDs. 0.1 g K30 PVP was mixed with 0.5 mL CDs solution, stirred for 10 min, and then the mixture was evenly mixed after ultrasonic treatment to obtain the CDs/PVP mixture. For the preparation of monochrome LED, by integrating green CDs/PVP composite material on the InGaN LED chip. For the preparation of other monochrome LED, the preparation method is the same as that of green LED, except that phosphor is different, that is, yellow and red CDs/PVP composite materials on the InGaN LED chip. Finally, these LEDs were placed directly in the oven and then cured at 80 °C for 1 h. After packaging, the prepared monochrome LED devices were measured in the integrating sphere. It is worth mentioning that the concentrations of the green, yellow, and red CDs' solutions were 0.644 mg/mL, 0.956 mg/mL, and 0.644 mg/mL, respectively.

120 KV transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-1400Plus/JEM-1400Plus) was used for morphology and structure characterization. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (ESCALAB 250Xi/ESCALAB 250Xi) was utilized to elemental and compositional analysis. X-ray diffractometer (XRD) was applied for lattice structure analysis. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Nicolet6700/Nicolet6700) and Raman spectra (LABHRev-UV) were utilized to composition and structure analysis. UV–visible near infrared spectrophotometer (Lambda 750 S/Lambda 750 S) was used to characterize the optical properties. The PL spectra of the LEDs were characterized by a Spectrascan Model PR-650 spectrophotometer with an integral sphere.

By one-step solvothermal method, using oPD as carbon source and nitrogen source and NiCl2 6H2O as Ni element dopant, green to red emission CDs were prepared at 180 °C by changing the solvent and reaction time. Metal Ni enhanced the fluorescence emission and redshift during the whole synthesis process. In order to verify this phenomenon, control experiments without Ni doping were carried out to ensure that the experimental conditions of undoped CDs are completely consistent with those of Ni-CDs. By dispersing the Ni-doped CDs and undoped CDs samples in the same solvent in the same proportion, it is obvious that the samples without metal doping only show green to yellow green fluorescence, while the samples with Ni doping show a wide range of adjustable fluorescence from green to red (Fig. 1 ). More importantly, the luminescence intensity of Ni-CDs is also significantly higher than that of undoped CDs.

The morphology and size of the as-prepared Ni-CDs (CDs1-DMF-1 h, CDs2-MeOH-3 h, CDs3-MeOH-13 h, CDs4-DMF-5 h, CDs5-DMF-20 h and CDs6-DMF-20 h 8) were characterized by TEM. Fig. 2 shows the low-resolution and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of CDs. The uniform distribution of CDs can be clearly seen in Fig. 2, indicating that the prepared Ni-CDs have good dispersion. The average particle size distribution of the Ni-CDs was approximately 2.01 nm, 2.21 nm, 2.31 nm, 3.16 nm, 5.33 nm, and 5.39 nm, respectively, corresponding to CDs1-DMF-1 h, CDs2-MeOH-3 h, CDs3-MeOH-13 h, CDs4-DMF-5 h, CDs5-DMF-20 h and CDs6-DMF-20 h 8, respectively. It should be mentioned that regarding those CDs are not in spherical shape, the corresponding histogram of the average size reported is based on the short axis. Through TEM image analysis, it can be clearly seen that with the extension of reaction time, the particle size of Ni-CDs increases, and their average size increases from the initial 2.01 nm–5.39 nm. This result indicates that there could be obvious quantum size effect since quantum size effect as proved to be one of the key factors to regulate optical properties. Therefore, Ni-CDs with different quantum sizes may lead to different fluorescence emission [47]. In addition, HRTEM images clearly reveal that all Ni-CDs have similar and distinguishable lattice fringes, and the lattice spacing is about 0.21 nm, which is in good agreement with the graphite carbon (100) diffraction plane [31]. It indicates that the core structure of the synthesized CDs is similar to graphite. At the same time, we also used XRD to measure these CDs to characterize the graphite structure of the CDs. As shown in Fig. S1, the CDs with six different colors all show a broad peak at about 25°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, which may be due to the highly disordered carbon [48]. The diffraction peak at around 42° corresponds to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon, which corresponds well to HRTEM. These peaks can well indicate that the prepared CDs have graphite properties [49].

XPS is a surface analysis technology, which is widely used to characterize the surface elements and chemical states of materials. Thus, the internal structure and possible surface groups of multicolor nickel-doped CDs (Ni-MCDs) were studied by XPS. As shown in Fig. 3 and Fig. S2, the XPS spectra of the Ni-MCDs (CDs1-DMF-1 h, CDs2-MeOH-3 h, CDs3-MeOH-13 h, CDs4-DMF-5 h, CDs5-DMF-20 h and CDs6-DMF-20 h 8) show four characteristic peaks at 286.08, 400.08, 532.08 and 856.08 eV, respectively, which correspond to C 1s, O 1s, N 1s and Ni 2p, indicating that all Ni-MCDs have the presence of the four elements mentioned above [13]. As shown in Fig. 3b, e, f and Figs. S2(b, e, f), the high-resolution C 1s spectra of Ni-MCDs can be fitted into three binding energy peaks of 284.3, 285.8 and 288 eV, corresponding to the presence of C=C/C–C, C–O/C–N and C=O groups by XPS high-resolution fine spectrum analysis, respectively [50,51]. Interestingly, it can be seen from the high-resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. 3) that the C=C/C–C peak from CDs1-DMF-1 h to CDs6-DMF-20 h 8 was significantly enhanced, which may be due to the increase of sp 2 carbon content, indicating that the graphite carbon degree of Ni-MCDs increases. The decrease of C–O/C–N peak indicates a decrease in sp 3 carbon content, while the increase of C=O peak indicates an increase in carbonyl carbon content. The peak fitting results of C 1s reveal that the crystalline quality of CDs may be improved from CDs1-DMF-1 h to CDs6-DMF-20 h 8. In addition, Fig. 3c, f, i and Figs. S2(c, f, i) show that the spectra of N 1s can be decomposed into three peaks at 398.5, 399.1 and 400.2 eV, respectively, which are attributed to pyridine N, pyrrole N and graphite N [41,52,53]. More importantly, it can be clearly observed from the high-resolution N 1s spectra in Fig. 3c, f, i and Figs. S2(c, f, i) that the content of graphite N increased significantly from CDs1-DMF-1 h to CDs6-DMF-20 h 8 samples. This proves that graphite N doping (at the center or edge) can greatly reduce the bandgap, i.e., they can significantly reduce the HOMO-LUMO gap, thus reducing the energy of the corresponding optical transition, resulting in a strong redshift [54]. What's more, it is obvious that the content of pyridine N decreases, but the increase of the content of pyridine N may lead to the capture of excitons and the change of surface states leading to a blueshift of fluorescence [25]. The synergistic effect of increasing graphite nitrogen content and decreasing pyridine N content can lead to PL redshift of CDs. As can be seen from Fig. S3, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s can be divided into 531.5 and 532.3 eV peaks which correspond to O=C and C–O–C [42,55]. From CDs1-DMF-1 h to CDs6-DMF-20 h 8, the O=C peak increases gradually, indicating that the surface oxidation degree increases. Finally, Fig. S4 shows that for the Ni 2p spectrum, the peaks at 854.2 and 871.6 eV can be attributed to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2, indicating the presence of Ni in the Ni-MCDs [56]. Two small peaks at 859.4 eV and 876.9 eV in the Ni 2p spectrum demonstrate the presence of trace nickel oxide due to exposure of the Ni-MCDs to air. These results indicate that the multicolor CDs prepared with the assistance of nickel ions are composed of π-π conjugated domains in the carbon core, and the distinct fluorescence may be caused by the different nitrogen doping amount [57]. As is shown in Fig. S5, the Raman spectra of the Ni-MCDs shown two obvious characteristic peaks, namely D peak and G peak, with corresponding peak positions of 1390 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1. The D peak can be attributed to defects such as hybrid disorder on the surface of Ni-MCDs, while the G peak can be attributed to the graphitization structure of Ni-MCDs [33,58,59]. The strength ratio of ID/IG is 1.08, 1.02, 1.01, 0.99, 0.92 and 0.90, respectively, indicating that the surface defects of Ni-MCDs decreases and the degree of graphitization increases.

In order to further verify the structure and configuration components of Ni-MCDs, FTIR was used to characterize the chemical structure of the compounds. FTIR spectra obtained from material analysis can reflect the types of functional groups contained in substances. The FTIR results of Ni-MCDs samples are shown in Fig. S6. Similar stretching vibration bands can be observed in the FTIR spectra of the Ni-MCDs samples, suggesting the existence of similar chemical components, such as –OH/NH at 3200–3500 cm−1, –CH2 at 2930 cm−1, C–C at 2853 cm−1, C–N at 1382 cm−1, C–O–C at 1096 cm−1, C=O at 1635 cm−1, and C=N at 1620 cm−1 [60]. The presence of C–H and C=N indicates the presence of pyridine N, and the presence of C–N and C=C tensile bonds confirms the presence of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons in Ni-MCDs. In addition, it can be clearly seen from the above FTIR spectra that there are multiple groups on the surface of Ni-MCDs, that is, these Ni-MCDs are all composed of aromatic structures containing –NH2, oxygen and nitrogen, and amino and carboxyl groups tend to form conjugated domain structures, resulting in many surface state emission traps. More importantly, because these CDs are rich in polar functional groups, they have good dispersibility in polar solvents. As shown in Fig. S7, excellent fluorescence can be observed by dispersing these CDs (CDs1-DMF-1 h to CDs6-DMF-20 h 8) in seven different solvents: ACE, DMSO, CYC, TOL, FOR, IPA and 1-BUT. It is found that these Ni-MCDs samples show different emission shifts in different solvents, which may be due to different surface states [61].

To further demonstrate the optical properties of the Ni-MCDs samples dispersed in ethanol, first of all, it can be intuitively found that the ethanol solutions exhibit different colors, showing tunable PL emission from green to red for the CDs (CDs1-DMF-1 h to CDs6-DMF-20 h 8). As shown in Fig. 4 a, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the Ni-MCDs (CDs1-DMF-1 h to CDs6-DMF-20 h 8) show obvious absorption in the ultraviolet and visible regions. In the ultraviolet region, it is obvious that there is a strongest absorption peak near 290 nm, which can be attributed to the π-π* transitions of C=C and C=N bonds in the carbon core. In addition, there is a secondary absorption peak near 420 nm, which can be attributed to the n-π transitions of C=O or C–N [34]. However, for CDs6-DMF-20 h 8, there is an obvious absorption peak at about 570 nm, which may come from some other functional groups on the surface of CDs6-DMF-20 h 8. In general, the Ni-MCDs showed several weak wide absorptions at 350–800 nm, which are considered to be broad absorptions on the surface of Ni-MCDs due to the large number of conjugate groups connected to the edges, indicating that these samples have different surface states, which are similar to many other reported CDs [62,63]. As shown in Fig. 4b and c, the optimum excitation and emission wavelengths are located at 440/513 nm, 450/529 nm, 429/537 nm, 449/579 nm, 492/596 nm, 570/612 nm, respectively. It is clear that the excitation and emission show an obvious redshift from CDs1-DMF-1 h to CDs6-DMF-20 h 8. In addition, the PL spectra have a wide coverage range, close to 200 nm. To better study the PL properties of Ni-MCDs, the excitation and emission of Ni-MCDs were systematically studied. As shown in Fig. S8, the Ni-MCDs show PL emission independent of excitation, that is, their PL emission peak does not change when the excitation wavelength is changed. However, it can be also found that the CDs4-DMF-5 h sample shows excitation dependence. As shown in Fig. S8d, the CDs4-DMF-5 h sample showed a wide PL peak under excitation at 400 nm. With the increase of excitation wavelength, the peak level at 540 nm gradually increased and the whole sample showed some redshift until the excitation wavelength increased to 470 nm. Interestingly, the PL shows a sharp peak and excitation independence at approximately 579 nm. This may be because different kinds of functional groups on the surface of CDs (such as C=O, C–O–C) cause a series of energy levels between π-π*, which may lead to many different emission sites. Under a specific excitation wavelength, a specific site will dominate, and the dominant emission site will change accordingly with the change of excitation wavelength. That is to say, it could be that the emission centers change, and the emission centers dominate differently under different excitation [64]. In order to study whether Ni doping enhances the fluorescence emission of CDs, the PL spectra of Ni-doped and undoped CDs were systematically studied. As shown in Fig. 4d–i, it can be clearly seen that the fluorescence intensity of Ni-MCDs is much higher than that of undoped CDs, and we can also obviously observe the PL redshift (tested at the same concentration). Meanwhile, we measured the quantum yield (QY) of Ni-MCDs and compared it with undoped CDs. As shown in Table S1, the QY of Ni-MCDs has a significant increase. More importantly, PL showed a significant redshift from CDs3-MeOH-13 h to CDs6-DMF-20 h 8. The absorption, excitation and emission spectrum of undoped CDs are shown in Fig. S9. The difference between Ni-CDs and undoped CDs is also fully shown in the optical pictures (Fig. S10). Whether in terms of PL intensity or luminescence color, the introduction of Ni obviously realizes the enhancement and redshift of fluorescence and peak position. At the same time, the time-resolved PL decay curves of these Ni-MCDs were also measured (Fig. S11). The results show that they can be fitted by a double exponential formula, including τ1 (short lifetime component) and τ2 (long lifetime component), which are assigned to nuclear state and surface state recombination processes, respectively [65]. From CDs1-DMF-1 h to CDs6-DMF-20 h 8, their average fluorescence lifetimes are 3.02 ns, 5.92 ns, 4.63 ns, 4.58 ns, 3.86 ns, and 4.27 ns, respectively (See Supplementary information for relevant calculations). All in all, it can be clearly detected that the fluorescence emission of Ni-MCDs has a significant redshift and fluorescence enhancement by optimizing the experimental conditions compared with undoped CDs. Through the analysis of the XPS and optical characterization results of the Ni-MCDs, it can be explained that the redshift emission is mainly attributed to the increase of sp 2 hybridization domain, the increase of graphitization degree and the increase of C=O group on the surface of CDs may also lead to PL redshift [66]. What's more, the optical results indicate that these different CDs have specific absorption structures and luminescent centers, that is the redshift of emission wavelength caused by prolonged reaction time is mainly related to enhanced passivation and increased graphite nitrogen content [67].

In order to measure the practical application value of Ni-MCDs, LEDs devices were manufactured to meet the needs. We mixed the prepared Ni-MCDs with PVP to obtain solid light-emission CDs/PVP composites. Three kinds of CDs were selected from Ni-MCDs, which were CDs1-DMF-1 h, CDs4-DMF-5 h and CDs6-DMF-20 h 8 respectively. As shown in Fig. S12, the prepared CDs/PVP composite films were continuously irradiated under 20 W UV lamp for 6 h, and it was found that the PL of the continuously irradiated composite films did not change compared with the unirradiated samples, which proves that our CDs/PVP composite films have remarkable optical stability properties. The CDs/PVP composites of CDs1-DMF-1 h and CDs4-DMF-5 h were coated on the UV 365 nm InGaN chip, the CDs/PVP composites of CDs6-DMF-20 h 8 were coated on the InGaN chip with 450 nm emission, and finally the monochrome green, yellow and red LED devices were prepared. As shown in Fig. 5 a–c, CIE and optical images of the LEDs made of corresponding CDs are presented. It can be clearly detected that the luminescence of the LEDs made of CDs are almost the same as that of the solution of CDs. The three LED devices have bright light at a constant 20 mA current, and their CIE coordinates are (0.37, 0.55), (0.47, 0.47), and (0.54, 0.30). In addition, PL spectra corresponding to three LEDs are shown in Fig. 5d–f. There are three obvious main emission peaks, corresponding to 539 nm, 581 nm and 626 nm respectively. However, regarding CDs1-DMF-1 h and CDs4-DMF-5 h, there is also a strong emission peak at 365 nm, while for CDs6-DMF-20 h 8, there is a strong emission peak at 450 nm. The appearance of these three peaks can be attributed to the influence of the chip itself. In order to measure the performance stability of the device, the PL under different currents was tested. The fluorescence intensity of each color LED device is different at the current of 20 mA, 40 mA, 60 mA, 80 mA, 100 mA and the voltage of 3 V (Fig. S13). For the green and yellow monochrome LED devices, their PL changes slightly with the change of current, but for red LED, PL basically does not change with the change of current. This indicates that Ni-CD6 has a good stability. Meanwhile, this also shows that Ni-MCDs for LED fabrication have a huge potential in the field of lighting.

In summary, a simple and efficient method has been developed for the synthesis of Ni-MCDs with enhanced and adjustable fluorescence emission by using oPD as a single precursor and NiCl2 6H2O as Ni metal dopant. With the help of Ni2+ and the time regulation of solvothermal reaction, the polymerization, crosslinking and carbonization degree of the precursor could be controlled, and the graphite nitrogen content and CDs size could be affected, so that the fluorescence emission of the prepared CDs could change from green to red. The fluorescence properties of Ni-doped and undoped CDs were systematically studied. It is found that the introduction of Ni could significantly enhance the fluorescence emission and the redshift of the peak position. In addition, the synthesized Ni-MCDs were easily dispersed in PVP to achieve solid-state luminescence and could be used to construct LEDs with different colors (green to red). The simplicity of the synthetic strategy and the successful manufacture of multicolor LEDs offer potential alternatives to actual lighting.

Yunhui Wu: Synthesis, Characterization, Writing – original manuscript, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Formal analysis. Jiurong Li: Characterization, Writing – original manuscript, Writing – review & editing. Xiujian Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Xiao Gong: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21774098), and the 111 project (No. B18038).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2022.09.060.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots with high fluorescence quantum yield: the fluorescence originates from organic fluorophores

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Shi, J.H. Yang, H.B. Zeng, Y.M. Chen, S.C. Yang, C. Wu, H. Zeng, O. Yoshihito, Q. Zhang, Carbon dots with high fluorescence quantum yield: the fluorescence originates from organic fluorophores, Nanoscale 8(30) (2016) 14374-14378.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in the rational synthesis of red-emissive carbon dots for nanomedicine applications: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Hallaji, Z. Bagheri, S.-O. Kalji, E. Ermis, B. Ranjbar, Recent advances in the rational synthesis of red-emissive carbon dots for nanomedicine applications: A review, FlatChem 29 (2021) 100271.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solvent-controlled synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots with a wide color gamut and narrowed emission peak widths

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ding, J.S. Wei, P. Zhang, Z.Y. Zhou, Q.Y. Gao, H.M. Xiong, Solvent-Controlled Synthesis of Highly Luminescent Carbon Dots with a Wide Color Gamut and Narrowed Emission Peak Widths, Small 14(22) (2018) e1800612.
